title: Dobos torte
subtitle: Hungarian seven-layer cake with chocolate buttercream and caramel
excerpt: A Hungarian celebration cake of seven thin genoise layers sandwiched with chocolate buttercream, topped with wedges of shattered caramel. Made in Budapest since 1885, it is a test of patience and knife skills rather than advanced technique.
sourceNotes: The Dobos torte was created by the Hungarian confectioner József Dobos in 1885 and presented at the Budapest National General Exhibition. Dobos released the recipe to the Budapest Confectioners' and Gingerbread Makers' Chamber of Commerce in 1906, making it public domain. The recipe is widely documented in Hungarian confectionery literature from the early twentieth century. The essential form of thin sponge layers, chocolate buttercream, and hard caramel top has remained unchanged. The recipe here follows the public-domain 1906 formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the caramel top. Thaw overnight in the fridge, then make the caramel on the day of serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble the buttercream layers up to two days ahead and refrigerate. Caramel the top on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. Each thin layer bakes in 7 to 9 minutes. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer is useful.

glossaryTerms.definition: The hard caramel layer that distinguishes a Dobos torte from every other layered cake. One sponge layer is set aside, cut into the number of portions the cake will serve, and each wedge is covered with hot caramel at the hard-crack stage (150°C) before the caramel sets. The wedges are then arranged at an angle on top of the buttercream-covered cake. If the caramel is poured onto all the slices at once and the cake cut later, the knife cracks the caramel unevenly.

content.text: A Dobos torte is assembled rather than baked in one go. Seven thin sponge rounds are baked separately, six are layered with chocolate buttercream, and one becomes the

. The caramel must be cooked to hard-crack stage, 150°C. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Work quickly once the caramel is poured — it sets in under a minute.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional. Draw seven 22 cm circles on baking paper and arrange on trays. Whisk the eggs and sugar with an electric mixer for 8 minutes until very thick and pale — the batter should fall from the whisk in a thick ribbon that holds its shape for 3 seconds. Sift the flour over the batter in two additions and fold in gently, then fold in the melted butter. Divide the batter evenly among the seven circles, spreading each one thin with a palette knife or icing spatula. Bake one or two at a time for 7 to 9 minutes until pale gold and just set. Cool on the trays.

Beat the butter and icing sugar until pale. Add the egg yolks and beat until smooth. Stir in the cooled melted chocolate until fully incorporated. Choose the best-looking sponge layer for the top and set it aside on a sheet of baking paper. Place the first sponge on a serving plate and spread with a thin, even layer of buttercream. Add the next layer and repeat. Stack all six layers, finishing with a layer of buttercream on the top and sides. Chill for 30 minutes.

Lightly oil the reserved sponge layer and a large knife. Combine the sugar and water in a small saucepan over medium heat. Do not stir once the sugar dissolves; brush the sides of the pan with a damp pastry brush if crystals form. Cook to hard-crack stage, 150°C on a sugar thermometer. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Working quickly, pour the caramel over the reserved sponge layer, tilting to coat evenly. As the caramel begins to set but before it hardens completely, cut the layer into 12 wedges with the oiled knife. Transfer each wedge to the top of the assembled cake, arranging them at a slight upward angle.
